Jul 15 2019

<div class="blog-header">

# Turn Short-Term Games Into Long-Term Games

<div class="blog-subtitle">

Improve your leverage by turning short-term relationships into long-term
ones

</div>

<div class="podcast-container style-1">

<div class="audio-player-container">

<div class="controls">

<div class="date">

Jul 15 2019

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="content">

**Nivi:**<span style=""> Do you want to talk about
</span>[<span style="">Pareto
optimal</span>](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pareto_efficiency)<span style="">?</span>

**Naval:**<span style=""> Pareto optimal is another concept from game
theory, along with Pareto superior.</span>

<span style="">Pareto superior means something is better in some ways
while being equal or better in other ways. It’s not worse in any way.
This is an important concept when you’re negotiating. If you can make a
solution Pareto superior to where it was before, you will always do
that. </span>

<span id="more-28493315"></span>

**Pareto optimal solutions require a trade-off to improve any
criterion**

<span style="">Pareto optimal is when the solution is the best it can
possibly be and you can’t change it without making it worse in at least
one dimension. There is a hard trade-off from this point
forward. </span>

<span style="">These are important concepts to understand when you’re
involved in a big negotiation. </span>

**Negotiations are won by whoever cares less**

<span style="">I generally say, though:
“</span>[<span style="">Negotiations are won by whoever cares
less</span>](https://twitter.com/naval/status/818630258916139008?lang=en)<span style="">.”
Negotiation is about not wanting it too badly. If you want something too
badly, the other person can extract more value from you. </span>

<span style="">If someone is taking advantage of you in a negotiation,
your best option is to turn it from a short-term game into a long-term
game. Try to make it a repeat game. Try to bring reputation into the
negotiation. Try to include other people who may want to play games with
this person in the future.</span>

<span style="">An example of a high-cost, low-information single-move
game is having your house renovated.</span>

<span style="">Contractors are notorious for overbooking, ripping people
off, and being unaccountable. I’m sure contractors have their own side
to it: “The homeowner has unreasonable demands.” “We found problems.”
“The homeowner doesn’t want to pay for it.” “They don’t understand;
they’re low-information buyers.”</span>

<span style="">It’s an expensive transaction. Historically it’s been
very hard to find good contractors; and the contractor has little
information on the homeowner.</span>

**Convert single-move games to multi-move games**

<span style="">So you try to go through friends. You try to find people
with good reputations. You’re converting an expensive single-move game
with a high probability of cheating on both sides into a multi-move
game. </span>

<span style="">One way to do that is to say: “Actually, I need two
different projects done. The first project we’ll do together, and based
on that I’ll decide if we do the second project.”</span>

<span style="">Another way is to say: “I’m going to do this project with
you, and I have three friends who want projects done who are waiting to
see the outcome of this project.”</span>

<span style="">Another way is to write a Yelp or Thumbtack
review—especially if the contractor  operates within a community and
wants to protect their reputation in that community.</span>

<span style="">These are all ways to turn a single-move game into a
longer term game and get past a position of poor negotiating leverage
and poor information.</span>

<div class="yarpp yarpp-related yarpp-related-website yarpp-template-yarpp-template-custom">

</div>

</div>
